beats.activity: Wake up, coffee, check weather and sourdough starter.; Light breakfast, shower, prepare for work.
beats.reasoning: Early riser habit; grey drizzly day means she'll adjust her outdoor plans and likely keep the morning routine shorter and cozier.

beats.activity: Short commute to pediatric practice in South Lake Union.
beats.reasoning: Rain + SR-520 closure doesn't affect Seattle-internal commute; she leaves early to avoid any delays and arrives calm for patient schedule.

beats.activity: Full patient schedule at pediatric dental practice: checkups, cleanings, minor procedures.
beats.reasoning: Tuesday morning is typically busy; rain outside means patients are more likely to keep appointments; she finds the work grounding.

beats.activity: Lunch break: grab a warm bowl and coffee at a nearby café, stay dry indoors.
beats.reasoning: Rain still falling until 2pm; she picks a familiar, cozy spot in the neighborhood rather than wandering; low energy (40/100 yesterday) means she conserves effort.

beats.activity: Afternoon patient schedule: more checkups and consultations.
beats.reasoning: Typical Tuesday afternoon at the practice; rain clearing after 2pm but she stays focused on work.
Rain has stopped; short walk/drive home; she's ready to decompress after a full day of patient care.

beats.activity: Feed and tend to sourdough starter; prep dinner ingredients.
beats.reasoning: Routine care of her sourdough; low energy from yesterday means she keeps dinner simple and grounding.
She cooks at home, a quiet ritual that pairs well with her introverted evening preference.

beats.activity: Work on knitting project and listen to a podcast or audiobook.; Evening wind-down: journal, review film negatives, prepare for bed.
beats.reasoning: Low energy continues; she spends the evening in quiet, meditative work with her hands—knitting is restorative and fits her mood.
Early riser needs solid rest; she'll wake up naturally around 5:30–6:00 am as usual, well-rested for Wednesday.

diary: Woke to that familiar grey Seattle drizzle—the kind that makes you want to stay under the covers. But the sourdough starter was calling, so I got up, fed it, and had my coffee while checking the weather. The commute was surprisingly smooth; I guess everyone else was stuck on the bridge mess. The practice was busy—lots of little mouths, lots of nervous energy, but that's what I love about this job. There's something about making a kid feel safe in the chair that never gets old. Grabbed a warm bowl at Molly Moon's to escape the rain for a bit, then back to it. By the time I got home, I was ready for quiet. Fed the starter again, prepped tomorrow's dinner, then just sat with my knitting and a podcast. The kind of evening that feels like pressing pause on the world.
thought_process: The bridge closure meant nothing to me today—I'm south of the lake anyway—but I was aware of the chaos out there, grateful for my short commute. The grey weather almost felt like permission to stay internal, focused on the small rituals: sourdough, knitting, film negatives. Work was steady and grounding; those kids keep me present in a way nothing else does. I chose to stay in at lunch rather than venture out into the rain, and that felt right—conservation of energy. By evening, I needed the slowness, the tactile work of needles and yarn. Mood-wise, I was calm but a bit contemplative, maybe touched by the weather. Nothing dramatic, just the quiet satisfaction of a day that held together.
world_event_prompt: Grey, drizzly Tuesday with steady rain from 7am to 2pm, high of 58F. SR-520 bridge closed eastbound 9am-noon for emergency repair, snarling cross-lake traffic.
